items.text: Do headings follow a logical hierarchy (h1 > h2 > h3) without skipping levels?

baseContent.whatItMeans: The page uses heading elements in sequential order without gaps, providing a meaningful document outline for assistive technologies.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Screen reader users navigate pages by headings. Skipped levels (e.g., h1 to h3) break the implied structure and make navigation confusing.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Use a browser extension like HeadingsMap to visualize the heading tree
- Check that no heading level is skipped
- Verify there is exactly one h1 per page
baseContent.exampleComment: This section jumps from h2 to h4 — the h3 level is skipped. Screen reader users navigating by heading will find this structure confusing.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Headings are the primary navigation mechanism for screen reader users — keep the hierarchy intact.

items.text: Are landmark regions (nav, main, aside, footer) used instead of generic divs?

baseContent.whatItMeans: The page structure uses HTML5 landmark elements so assistive technologies can identify and navigate to major page regions.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Landmarks let screen reader users jump directly to navigation, main content, or complementary sections without tabbing through everything.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that the page has a <main> element wrapping primary content
- Verify navigation uses <nav> elements
- Ensure there are no generic div wrappers where landmarks would be appropriate
baseContent.exampleComment: This navigation bar is wrapped in a <div>. Using <nav> instead gives screen reader users a landmark to jump to.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Landmark elements provide a navigational skeleton that screen reader users rely on.

items.text: Are interactive elements using the correct semantic element (button for actions, anchor for navigation)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Buttons trigger in-page actions, anchors navigate to a URL. Using the right element ensures correct keyboard behavior and screen reader announcements.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A div with an onClick handler has no keyboard support or role announcement by default. Using the wrong element forces workarounds and confuses users.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Search for div or span elements with onClick handlers
- Verify buttons are used for in-page actions (modals, toggles, form submits)
- Verify anchors are used for navigation with valid href attributes
baseContent.exampleComment: This <div onClick={handleDelete}> should be a <button>. Divs aren't focusable or announced as interactive by screen readers.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use button for actions and anchor for navigation — never a div with a click handler.

items.text: Are groups of related items marked up as lists (ul, ol, dl)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Related items like navigation links, feature lists, or menu options use proper list elements so assistive technologies can convey the grouping and count.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Screen readers announce list length (e.g., 'list, 5 items'), helping users decide whether to explore or skip. Divs with visual-only grouping lose this context.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Look for sets of visually similar items that are not wrapped in ul/ol
- Check navigation menus for proper list markup
- Verify definition lists are used for key-value pairs where appropriate
baseContent.exampleComment: These navigation links are rendered as sibling divs. Wrapping them in a <ul> lets screen readers announce the group and item count.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Lists convey structure and count to assistive technologies — use them for grouped items.

items.text: Does every form input have an associated label element or accessible name?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Every input, select, and textarea has a visible <label> linked via for/id or wrapping, or at minimum an aria-label or aria-labelledby attribute.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Unlabeled inputs are announced as just 'edit text' by screen readers. Users cannot determine what information to enter, making the form unusable.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check each form control for a <label> with a matching for attribute
- Verify placeholder text is not the only label
- Run an automated scan (axe, Lighthouse) for missing labels
baseContent.exampleComment: This email input relies on placeholder text as its only label. Placeholders disappear on focus and aren't reliably announced. Add a <label> element.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Every form control needs a programmatically associated label — placeholders are not labels.

items.text: Are data tables using proper th, caption, and scope attributes?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Data tables use <th> for headers with scope attributes, and optionally <caption> to describe the table's purpose.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Screen readers use table headers to announce cell context. Without proper markup, users hear raw data with no idea which column or row it belongs to.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that data tables use <th> elements for column and row headers
- Verify scope='col' or scope='row' is set on th elements
- Ensure layout tables are not used (use CSS grid/flexbox instead)
baseContent.exampleComment: This table uses <td> for all cells including headers. The first row should use <th scope='col'> so screen readers can associate data cells with their column.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Proper table markup lets screen readers announce row and column context for each cell.

items.text: Is ARIA only used when native HTML semantics are insufficient?

baseContent.whatItMeans: ARIA attributes are a last resort — native HTML elements (button, nav, dialog) already provide the correct roles and behaviors.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Incorrect ARIA is worse than no ARIA. It overrides native semantics and creates a confusing experience. Native HTML elements are better supported and tested.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Look for role='button' on elements that could be <button>
- Check for aria-label on elements that already have visible text labels
- Verify ARIA roles match the element's actual behavior
baseContent.exampleComment: This <div role='button'> should just be a <button>. The native element gives you keyboard support and correct announcements for free.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: The first rule of ARIA: don't use ARIA if a native HTML element will do the job.

items.text: Are dynamic content updates announced via aria-live regions?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Content that changes without a page reload (toasts, form errors, status updates) uses aria-live to notify screen readers of the change.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Screen readers only read content on initial load or focus. Without aria-live, dynamic updates like success messages or error alerts are completely invisible.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Identify all dynamic content areas (notifications, loading states, inline errors)
- Verify they use aria-live='polite' or aria-live='assertive'
- Check that the live region exists in the DOM before content is injected
baseContent.exampleComment: This toast notification appears visually but isn't announced by screen readers. Wrap it in an aria-live='polite' region.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: If content changes dynamically and users need to know, use an aria-live region.

items.text: Do expandable elements use aria-expanded to communicate their state?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Buttons that toggle visibility of content (accordions, dropdowns, menus) use aria-expanded to indicate whether the content is currently shown or hidden.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without aria-expanded, screen reader users cannot tell whether clicking a toggle will show or hide content, leading to disorientation.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Find all toggle/expand buttons in the PR
- Verify they set aria-expanded='true' or 'false' based on state
- Check that aria-controls points to the ID of the controlled element
baseContent.exampleComment: This dropdown toggle button doesn't use aria-expanded. Screen reader users can't tell whether the menu is open or closed.

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code: ...

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always pair toggle buttons with aria-expanded to communicate the current state.

items.text: Do modal dialogs use role='dialog' with aria-modal and proper focus management?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Modals are marked with role='dialog' and aria-modal='true', focus is trapped inside while open, and focus returns to the trigger element on close.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without proper modal semantics, screen reader users can navigate behind the modal to obscured content. Without focus trapping, keyboard users can tab into invisible elements.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Open the modal and try tabbing — focus should cycle within the modal
- Press Escape — the modal should close and focus should return to the trigger
- Verify role='dialog' and aria-modal='true' are set
- Check for an aria-labelledby pointing to the modal title
baseContent.exampleComment: This modal doesn't trap focus. A keyboard user can tab past the modal into the background page. Use a focus trap and add aria-modal='true'.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Modals must trap focus, announce themselves as dialogs, and return focus on close.

items.text: Do icon-only buttons and image links have accessible names (aria-label or visually hidden text)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Interactive elements that rely solely on an icon for their visual label must have a text alternative via aria-label, aria-labelledby, or visually hidden text.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Screen readers announce icon-only buttons as just 'button' with no context. Users have no way to know what the button does.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Find all icon-only buttons and image links
- Verify each has an aria-label or sr-only text
- Check that the label describes the action, not the icon (e.g., 'Close' not 'X icon')
baseContent.exampleComment: This icon button is announced as just 'button' by screen readers. Add aria-label='Close dialog' to describe its purpose.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Every interactive element needs a text name — icons alone are invisible to screen readers.

items.text: Is aria-hidden='true' used correctly and not hiding focusable content?

baseContent.whatItMeans: aria-hidden='true' removes elements from the accessibility tree. It must not be placed on elements that contain focusable children, as this creates invisible focus traps.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Hiding focusable content from the accessibility tree means screen reader users can still tab to it but hear nothing, creating a confusing dead zone.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Search for aria-hidden='true' in the diff
- Verify none of those elements contain buttons, links, or inputs
- Check that decorative icons use aria-hidden='true' appropriately
baseContent.exampleComment: This container has aria-hidden='true' but contains a focusable link. The link becomes an invisible focus trap for screen reader users.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Never apply aria-hidden to containers with focusable children — it creates invisible focus traps.

items.text: Does the tab order follow a logical reading sequence?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Pressing Tab moves focus through interactive elements in an order that matches the visual layout and reading flow of the page.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A confusing tab order disorients keyboard users. If focus jumps unpredictably, users cannot build a mental model of the page.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Tab through the page and note the focus order
- Verify it matches the visual layout (left-to-right, top-to-bottom for LTR languages)
- Check for positive tabindex values that override natural order
baseContent.exampleComment: Focus jumps from the header directly to the footer, skipping the main content. Check for tabindex values that are overriding the natural DOM order.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Avoid positive tabindex values — let the DOM order determine tab sequence.

items.text: Is a visible focus indicator present on all interactive elements?

baseContent.whatItMeans: When an interactive element receives keyboard focus, it displays a clearly visible outline or ring so the user knows where they are on the page.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without a focus indicator, keyboard users lose track of their position on the page. This is a WCAG 2.1 AA requirement (2.4.7 Focus Visible).
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Tab through all interactive elements
- Verify each shows a visible focus ring or outline
- Check CSS for 'outline: none' or 'outline: 0' without a replacement style
baseContent.exampleComment: This button has outline: none with no replacement focus style. Keyboard users can't tell when it's focused. Add a :focus-visible style.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Never remove focus outlines without providing an equally visible replacement.

items.text: Can all functionality be operated with a keyboard alone?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Every action available via mouse (clicking, hovering, dragging) has a keyboard equivalent. No functionality is locked behind mouse-only interactions.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Users who cannot use a mouse (motor impairments, screen reader users) are completely blocked if an action requires mouse interaction.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Try completing all user flows using only the keyboard
- Check that hover-triggered content (tooltips, menus) can also be triggered via focus or keyboard
- Verify drag-and-drop has a keyboard alternative
baseContent.exampleComment: This drag-and-drop reorder only works with a mouse. Keyboard users need an alternative — consider up/down arrow keys with screen reader announcements.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: If you can't do it with a keyboard, some users can't do it at all.

items.text: Is a 'skip to main content' link provided?

baseContent.whatItMeans: A hidden link at the top of the page becomes visible on focus and allows keyboard users to jump past repetitive navigation directly to the main content.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without a skip link, keyboard users must tab through the entire navigation on every page load. This is tedious and time-consuming.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Press Tab on page load — a 'Skip to content' link should appear
- Verify activating it moves focus to the <main> element
- Check that the link is visually hidden until focused
baseContent.exampleComment: There's no skip link on this page. Keyboard users have to tab through 15 nav items to reach the main content on every page.

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
Skip to main content
...

baseContent.keyTakeaway: A skip link saves keyboard users from tabbing through navigation repeatedly.

items.text: Are there any keyboard traps where focus gets stuck?

baseContent.whatItMeans: A keyboard trap occurs when a user tabs into a component and cannot tab out again. Focus should always be escapable except in intentional modal dialogs.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Keyboard traps completely block users from navigating the rest of the page. This is a critical WCAG failure (2.1.2 No Keyboard Trap).
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Tab into every interactive component (custom widgets, embedded content, iframes)
- Verify you can tab out in both directions
- Check that modals can be closed with Escape
baseContent.exampleComment: Focus gets trapped inside this custom datepicker widget. Once a keyboard user tabs in, they can't tab out. Add keyboard handlers to allow exiting.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Users must always be able to move focus away from any component using standard keyboard interactions.

items.text: Do custom interactive widgets support expected keyboard patterns (arrows, Enter, Escape)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Custom components like tabs, menus, and comboboxes implement the keyboard patterns defined in WAI-ARIA Authoring Practices (arrow keys to navigate, Enter to select, Escape to close).
baseContent.whyItMatters: Users expect consistent keyboard behavior based on the component's role. A tab panel that doesn't respond to arrow keys forces users to tab through every tab, breaking the expected interaction.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Reference the WAI-ARIA Authoring Practices for the component type
- Test arrow key navigation within the widget
- Verify Enter/Space activates and Escape dismisses where appropriate
baseContent.exampleComment: This custom tab component requires Tab to move between tabs instead of arrow keys. ARIA tab patterns expect left/right arrow key navigation.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Custom widgets should follow WAI-ARIA Authoring Practices keyboard patterns for their role.

items.text: Does text meet WCAG AA color contrast ratios (4.5:1 normal, 3:1 large)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Normal-sized text has at least a 4.5:1 contrast ratio against its background, and large text (18pt+ or 14pt bold+) has at least 3:1.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Insufficient contrast makes text unreadable for users with low vision, color blindness, or anyone using a screen in bright sunlight. This is one of the most common accessibility failures.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Use a contrast checker tool (WebAIM Contrast Checker, axe DevTools)
- Test all text colors against their backgrounds
- Pay special attention to gray text on white backgrounds and text over images
baseContent.exampleComment: This #999 text on #fff background has a contrast ratio of 2.85:1, well below the 4.5:1 minimum. Consider #767676 or darker.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: 4.5:1 for normal text, 3:1 for large text — no exceptions for AA compliance.

items.text: Is color NOT the only means of conveying information?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Information conveyed by color is also conveyed through text, icons, patterns, or other visual cues. Color-blind users must be able to understand the UI without color.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Approximately 8% of men and 0.5% of women have some form of color vision deficiency. Red/green status indicators alone exclude these users.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check error states — are they indicated by more than just red text?
- Check charts and graphs — do they use patterns or labels in addition to colors?
- View the page in grayscale to verify information is still conveyed
baseContent.exampleComment: Required fields are indicated only by a red border. Color-blind users can't distinguish this. Add an asterisk or text label as well.

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code: ⚠️ Error: This field is required

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always supplement color with text, icons, or patterns to convey information.

items.text: Does the layout remain usable when text is resized to 200%?

baseContent.whatItMeans: When users zoom to 200% or increase their browser's default font size, text reflows correctly without being clipped, truncated, or overlapping other elements.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Low-vision users rely on text resizing. If text overflows containers or overlaps other content, the page becomes unusable. WCAG requires content to be usable at 200% zoom.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Zoom the browser to 200% and test all pages
- Check that text is not clipped by overflow: hidden containers
- Verify no horizontal scrolling is introduced
- Ensure buttons and inputs grow to accommodate larger text
baseContent.exampleComment: At 200% zoom, the button text overflows the button container because it has a fixed height. Use min-height and padding instead.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use relative units and flexible containers so content reflows cleanly at 200% zoom.

items.text: Are animations reduced or removed when prefers-reduced-motion is set?

baseContent.whatItMeans: The code checks the prefers-reduced-motion media query and disables or reduces animations for users who have enabled this OS-level preference.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Animations can cause vestibular disorders, motion sickness, and seizures in sensitive users. Respecting their preference is both an accessibility requirement and a safety concern.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Enable 'Reduce motion' in OS accessibility settings
- Verify animations are minimized or replaced with instant transitions
- Check CSS for @media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce) rules
baseContent.exampleComment: This parallax scroll effect doesn't respect prefers-reduced-motion. Users with vestibular disorders may experience dizziness. Add a reduced-motion media query.

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always check prefers-reduced-motion and provide a reduced or no-animation alternative.

items.text: Are touch targets at least 44x44 CSS pixels?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Interactive elements like buttons and links have a minimum tap/click area of 44x44 CSS pixels, meeting WCAG 2.5.5 Target Size requirements.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Small touch targets are difficult to activate for users with motor impairments, tremors, or anyone using a touchscreen. This also improves usability for all users on mobile.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Inspect interactive elements and verify their clickable area is at least 44x44px
- Check inline links in dense text for adequate spacing
- Verify icon buttons have sufficient padding
baseContent.exampleComment: This icon button is 24x24 pixels. The clickable area should be at least 44x44px — add padding to increase the touch target.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: 44x44 CSS pixels minimum for any interactive element — use padding if the icon is smaller.

items.text: Does content reflow at 320px viewport width without horizontal scrolling?

baseContent.whatItMeans: At a viewport width of 320 CSS pixels (equivalent to 400% zoom on a 1280px screen), all content reflows into a single column without requiring horizontal scrolling.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Users who zoom significantly or use narrow viewports need content to reflow. Horizontal scrolling combined with vertical scrolling makes content extremely difficult to read.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Set viewport width to 320px in DevTools
- Verify no horizontal scroll bar appears
- Check that all content is visible and readable
- Ensure no text is cut off or overlapping
baseContent.exampleComment: This fixed-width table forces horizontal scrolling at 320px. Consider a responsive card layout or allow the table to stack vertically on narrow viewports.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Content should reflow to a single column at 320px — no horizontal scrolling.

items.text: Do meaningful images have descriptive alt text, and decorative images have empty alt?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Images that convey information have alt text describing their content or function. Decorative images use alt='' to be skipped by screen readers.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Missing alt text causes screen readers to announce the filename (e.g., 'IMG_2847.jpg'), which is useless. Decorative images without empty alt create noise.
baseContent.howToVerify: - Check every for an alt attribute - Verify informative images have descriptive alt text - Verify decorative images have alt='' - Check SVG icons for aria-hidden='true' or role='img' with a title
baseContent.exampleComment: This product image has no alt text. Screen reader users will hear the filename instead of a description. Add alt='Blue running shoes, side view'.

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Meaningful images need descriptive alt; decorative images need empty alt to be silent.

items.text: Does each page have a unique, descriptive title?

baseContent.whatItMeans: The document <title> element is unique for each page and describes the page's content or purpose, typically in the format 'Page Name - Site Name'.
baseContent.whyItMatters: The page title is the first thing announced when a screen reader loads a page. It also appears in browser tabs and bookmarks, helping users identify pages.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that the <title> element is set for each route
- Verify it changes when navigating between pages in an SPA
- Ensure the title describes the page content, not just the site name
baseContent.exampleComment: All pages share the same title 'MyApp'. Each page should have a descriptive title like 'Dashboard - MyApp' or 'Settings - MyApp'.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Each page should have a unique, descriptive title that is announced on page load.

items.text: Is the lang attribute set on the html element?

baseContent.whatItMeans: The <html> element has a lang attribute specifying the page's primary language, and content in other languages uses lang attributes on their containing elements.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Screen readers use the lang attribute to select the correct speech synthesizer and pronunciation rules. Without it, content may be read in the wrong language.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check the <html> tag for a lang attribute (e.g., lang='en')
- Verify content in other languages has lang overrides
- Ensure the lang value matches the actual content language
baseContent.exampleComment: The <html> element is missing a lang attribute. Screen readers may default to the wrong language pronunciation. Add lang='en' (or the appropriate language).
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Set lang on <html> so screen readers use the correct pronunciation.

items.text: Are form validation errors announced to screen readers and associated with their fields?

baseContent.whatItMeans: When form validation fails, errors are programmatically associated with their input via aria-describedby, and focus moves to the first error or an error summary.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Sighted users see red error messages. Screen reader users need errors to be announced and linked to the correct field, or they won't know what's wrong.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Submit a form with invalid data
- Verify errors are announced by screen readers
- Check that each error uses aria-describedby linked to its input
- Verify focus moves to the error summary or first invalid field
baseContent.exampleComment: This validation error appears visually below the input but isn't linked with aria-describedby. Screen reader users won't hear the error when focused on the field.

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code: Invalid email address

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Link errors to inputs with aria-describedby and announce them with role='alert' or aria-live.

items.text: Does the DOM order match the visual reading order?

baseContent.whatItMeans: The order of elements in the HTML source matches how they appear visually. CSS reordering (flexbox order, grid, absolute positioning) doesn't create a mismatch.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Screen readers follow DOM order, not visual order. If CSS reorders content, screen reader users experience the page in a different sequence than sighted users.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Compare the DOM order with the visual layout
- Check for CSS order property usage in flexbox/grid
- Verify absolutely positioned elements aren't creating reading order mismatches
baseContent.exampleComment: CSS flexbox order moves the sidebar before the main content visually, but the DOM has main content first. Screen readers will read main content first, creating a mismatch.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Keep DOM order consistent with visual order — avoid CSS reordering that breaks reading flow.

items.text: Are loading states and progress updates communicated to screen readers?

baseContent.whatItMeans: When content is loading, screen readers are informed via aria-busy, role='status', or aria-live regions. Progress bars use role='progressbar' with aria-valuenow.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Sighted users see spinners and progress bars. Without announcements, screen reader users may think nothing is happening or that the page is broken.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Trigger loading states and listen with a screen reader
- Check for aria-busy='true' on regions that are loading
- Verify progress bars have role='progressbar' with value attributes
- Check that completion is announced
baseContent.exampleComment: This spinner gives no screen reader feedback. Add an aria-live='polite' region with 'Loading...' text, and update it when loading completes.

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code: Loading results...

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Every visual loading indicator needs an equivalent announcement for screen readers.
